name: Greek Cosmology
shortDescription: Greek cosmology describes the structure and origin of the universe as understood by the ancient Greeks, encompassing their beliefs about the heavens, earth, and underworld.
description: Greek cosmology offers a fascinating glimpse into the ancient Greek understanding of the universe. Initially, the world was believed to have emerged from Chaos, a primordial void. From Chaos arose Gaia (Earth), Tartarus (the Underworld), Eros (Desire), Erebus (Darkness), and Nyx (Night). Gaia then gave birth to Uranus (Sky), who became her consort. Their union produced the Titans, who ruled until overthrown by the Olympian gods. The Olympian gods, led by Zeus, then established order, dividing the cosmos into domains: Zeus ruled the sky, Poseidon the sea, and Hades the underworld. The Earth was conceived as a flat disk surrounded by the River Oceanus. Above was the bronze vault of the sky, populated by the sun, moon, and stars, which were often seen as deities or associated with them (Helios the sun, Selene the moon). Below lay Tartarus, a dark and abyssal place of punishment. The concept of the cosmos evolved over time, with philosophers like Anaximander and Pythagoras proposing more sophisticated models, including a spherical Earth and celestial spheres. Despite these advancements, the mythological framework remained influential, shaping Greek art, literature, and religious practices. The Judgment of Paris, for instance, reflects the hierarchical order of the cosmos, with deities vying for power and influence within their respective domains.
